data.title: Hess As The Asset, Chevron As The Platform: A Merger Built For The Future
data.description:
Introduction

Today, Hess (NYSE:HES) is not just an industry pioneer, but a dramatically reformed player who has gone through a transformation from a vertically integrated oil giant with its own refineries and a network of filling stations to a clean, focused E&P company. Hess's entire business is based on mining, and its assets include the Bakken shale in the United States, and especially the Guyana Hess, where the company has a 30% stake in the mega-discovery, which, in fact, has become one of the largest in the last ten years.

Management moved the entire infrastructure to a separate company, Hess Midstream, which gave the company flexibility, control and stable income. 2024 was an important year for the company. The Payara project in Guyana was launched, which resulted in an increase in production by as much as 52%. But the main news of the year is, of course, the deal with Chevron. This is a real big deal: Hess shareholders move under the wing of a major and gain access to a completely different level of dividend yield.

Business model

The company earns its main cash through the extraction and sale of oil, condensate and gas. It sells primarily to processors and traders — part of the supply goes through spot, part under long contracts. For example, in 2024 alone, Hess shipped 59 tanker shipments of oil from Guyana, and each of them made a significant contribution to revenue. Gas is a different story: in the United States, it is either processed or used for the needs of the fields themselves, while in Southeast Asia it is supplied to power plants.

According to the revenue structure, approximately 80-85% is accounted for by oil, condensate and CFLU, and the remaining 15-20% is accounted for by gas. The company's portfolio includes low-sulfur oil from Guyana and shale from Bakken. The average selling price of oil in 2024 was about $77 per barrel.

It is worth mentioning separately about the midstream through which Hess services its production: it collects, transports and partially processes oil and gas, mainly in North Dakota. This segment is output in Hess Midstream LP. In 2024 alone, Hess paid domestic tariffs worth about $1.23 billion, and in return received stable service and dividends from a stake in midstream, while not meeting its debts.

If you rewind back, Hess used to be a classic all-in-one player: refineries, gas stations, trading. But starting in 2013, a reboot began. Hess sold all non—core assets — refineries, a network of gas stations, trading - and raised $13 billion, which it used to pay off debts and restart the business, already as a purely mining company. Since then, the strategy has been simple and straightforward: focus on high-yielding assets. For example, deposits in Europe and Asia were sold, and the proceeds were sent to Guyana, which became a "gold mine" for the company.

The creation of Hess Midstream is another strong move. Instead of selling the infrastructure to some fund, Hess organized a joint venture with GIP, conducted an IPO of a portion of the stake, and now uses this platform as a stable cache generator without diverting capital costs to it. The entire Bakken expansion is funded by midstream itself, while Hess simply pays a fixed tariff.

The Chevron Deal

The deal between Hess and Chevron is a turning point in the company's history. Chevron (CVX) is buying back 100% of Hess in a stock exchange format: 1,025 Chevron shares for each Hess share, without a cash surcharge. This gave Hess shareholders a small premium to the market (5%) and the opportunity to receive about 14% in the combined company. Hess CEO, John Hess, will join Chevron's board, and the deal is expected to close in the first half of 2025, after arbitration with Exxon and CNOOC is resolved.

Strategically, Chevron gains control of a stake in a giant offshore project in Guyana, adding +1.4 billion barrels into reserves and increasing production by 10%. The advantage is the diversification of the shale portfolio due to Bakken and access to midstream infrastructure. Hess shareholders, in turn, get access to a sustainable business, global scale, a more generous dividend policy (3-4% versus the previous 1.3%) and the liquidity of Chevron shares. The synergy from the merger promises savings of $100 million, and the entire Hess debt will be transferred to Chevron's balance sheet with a higher rating. In fact, Hess gives away growth, getting scale and stability in return.

Competitors

As I said, Hess works in upstream. There is competition for licenses, for access to mineral resources, for capital, and for the speed of project implementation. In 2024, the oil market is about $3 trillion and consumption is about 102-103 million barrels per day. And despite all the talk about energy transition, demand is still creeping up - slowly, with a global CAGR of about 0.7% until 2030, but steadily. Gas is the same story: after 2020, the market returned to a level above 4 trillion cubic meters. The scale is gigantic, but the organic growth is restrained.

Against this background, Hess is not a giant at all: only 0.5% of global production, or about 481 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day. This is 7 times less than Exxon Mobil (XOM) and 23 times less than Aramco (ARMCO). But in terms of his weight, among independent E&P, Hess is a serious player. Hess has a multi-level competition. Hess intersects with majors like Exxon or Chevron in projects like Guyana and the Gulf of Mexico. With ConocoPhillips (COP), Marathon Oil (MPC), this is already a fight on equal terms: for the investor, for assets, for efficiency. National companies like Aramco do not compete directly with Hess, but through OPEC+ and the capital market they influence her life no less.

The industry has shaken up a lot in recent years. In 2020— there will be a collapse in demand, Brent will fall below $20, Hess is in the red by $3.1 billion. Already in 2021, a rebound began, and in 2022, a boom: oil at $100+, a record profit of $2.1 billion. This gave rise to a wave of transactions and consolidation. In 2023-24— there will be a lull, prices are $70-85, moderate growth, everyone is busy trying not to overheat budgets. During this time, Hess continued to rock due to organic growth — mainly due to Guyana, of course.

But 2025 brought a new twist — Trump's tariff policy. On the one hand, Trump's previous rhetoric – support for fossil fuels, the slogan "Drill, baby, drill" – suggests deregulation of production, simplification of obtaining licenses, and the lifting of climate restrictions, which is theoretically favorable for oil companies. However, the introduction of high tariffs on goods (especially metal products and equipment) and trade wars negatively affect the industry indirectly.

Trade conflicts are reducing global economic growth, and hence the demand for oil. If the administration imposes harsh tariffs against major economies (China, the EU), this leads to retaliatory measures, slowing down trade and industry, which negatively affects oil prices – after the tariffs were announced, the price of Brent fell to $64. For companies, such a price shock is a serious factor: at < $60, many shale projects in The United States are becoming barely profitable. With regard to Hess: if oil in 2025 does not remain at the expected $70+, but falls into the range of $ 50-60 due to trade wars, Hess revenue and profit will be lower than forecast. For example, at $55/bbl instead of $75 budget, Hess revenue could decrease by 25%. Accordingly, the effect of tariffs is to reduce the income of upstream companies.

If you put all this into a picture, then for Hess the background is rather neutral-positive. Yes, there are enough risks. But the company has built a stable position: a clear portfolio, strong assets, and a partnership with a major company. Consolidation is underway, and Hess has fit into it competently. And now it's moving into the next phase, as part of Chevron.

Financials

After a minimum revenue of $4.54 billion in 2020 amid a collapse in prices and falling production, the company quickly recovered: in 2021, it grew to $7.3 billion, in 2022, it soared to $11.07 billion amid expensive oil and the launch of the second FPSO in Guyana. In 2023, revenue decreased slightly to $10.3 billion due to falling prices, despite increased production. But 2024 has become strong again: $12.66 billion due to the growth of physical volume and high sales of oil from Guyana.

With this in mind, the 5-year revenue CAGR is 23%, but this is the recovery effect after 2020. I expect growth to slow down in 2025.: revenue, according to my model, will reach $12.85 billion (+1.5% by 2024) — volumes will grow due to Guyana and a stable Bakken, but falling oil prices will offset this effect. The company did not publish a formal forecast due to the merger with Chevron, but the calculations show that Hess is reaching a revenue plateau in the region of $12-13 billion, and further growth is possible only with an improved market or the launch of new FPSOs in 2026-27. My forecast is generally consistent with the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $12.01-13.01 billion.

Hess has steadily increased operational efficiency in recent years, and EBITDA clearly reflects this. After a loss—making year in 2020, when EBITDA was negative, the company achieved steady growth. 2024 was a record year: EBITDA reached $7.3 billion with an outstanding margin of 57.7% - the result of increased production in Guyana, declining unit economy and no write-downs.

By 2025, I expect a moderate decrease to $6.59 billion in EBITDA and a margin of about 51.2%. The reason is lower oil prices (an average of $70 versus $77 in 2024), while costs will remain stable. Nevertheless, this is still a very high profitability by industry standards: the average margin for independent miners is 45%. Thanks to the quality of assets, primarily Guyanese, Hess maintains a strong position in the upper segment of the industry.

By 2024, Hess Corporation's debt position looked stable. The company had $9.46 billion in debt on its balance sheet, with $1.2 billion in cash, and net debt was only $8.28 billion, less than 1.15x EBITDA. It is important that the main part of the liabilities is concentrated at the parent company level, while the debt of Hess Midstream ($3.5 billion) has no impact on Hess Corp, reducing risks for shareholders. The company has consistently reduced its debt burden: Debt/Capital fell from 1.97x in 2020 to 1.21x in 2024. The debt is based on bonds maturing after 2027 with comfortable rates of 3-6.25%, and a credit line of $3.5 billion has hardly been selected. At the same time, interest expenses in 2024 amounted to about $412 million, with more than 11 times coverage.

In 2024, Hess reached the peak of the investment cycle: capital expenditures reached almost $4.95 billion, accounting for a record 39% of revenue. The main focus was on accelerated financing of Guyanese projects – the company bought out shares in FPSO Liza Destiny and Prosperity ahead of schedule, and also advanced through new phases (Uaru, Whiptail). At the same time, drilling in the Bakken was supported. In my estimation, 2025 will be a transitional year. I expect CapEx to decrease to $4.1 billion, and the CapEx-to-revenue ratio to 32%. The decrease is due to the completion of large purchases, the launch of FPSO Payara and a decrease in the intensity of spending on some phases. Even in a more aggressive scenario, CapEx/Revenue is unlikely to exceed 35%. At the same time, I expect that the volume of drilling in the United States will remain stable, and in Guyana, the costs will be distributed more evenly.

In 2024, against the background of high capital expenditures ($4.9 billion) and moderate revenue, FCF amounted to only $681 million. This reflects the peak investment phase: the company was actively building an FPSO and expanding in Guyana. However, in 2025, Hess, according to my calculations, will reach a qualitatively new level — FCF will grow to $2.75 billion, which is equivalent to 21% of revenue. The reasons are the reduction of CapEx to $4.1 billion and the continued high operating profit. Formally, this reflects the company's transition from the stage of intensive growth to the phase of monetary generation. In other words, Hess completes the largest investments and begins to accumulate resources.

In fiscal year 2024, the company paid dividends of $577 million, or $1.88 per share, with a yield of about 1.51%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $607 million for dividends or $1.98 per share with a yield of 1.54%.

Valuation

The company's shares have adjusted strongly by 18.49% over the past year, with the S&P500 rising by 3.16%. The drop in quotations is due to a slowdown in the growth of financial indicators and, in general, the decline in the entire stock market after Trump's April statements on tariffs. I believe that such a strong drop in price has created an upside in price of 5-6%, which can be used.

The company is trading at a discount to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the P/E premium is 44%, and the EV/EBITDA premium is about 26%.

Compared to peers in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, the company is also trading at discounts, as can be seen in the table below. Hess looks undervalued relative to the average values among competitors: the company's P/E is 14.31x, which is significantly lower than the industry average of 31.16x, P/B is slightly lower than the average, and EV/EBITDA is also almost 2 times lower than the average and amounts to 6.64x. At the same time, the company is one of the most profitable among peers, and in terms of revenue growth over the past 5 years, it has been ahead of all competitors.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), target value of P/E, P/S and EV/EBITDA with estimated weights of 20%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 7.33%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 16.54x, P/S is 3.16x, and EV/EBITDA is 8.76x (took the consensus forecast).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $135.47 with a growth potential of 5.42% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 6.96%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. Wall Street analysts are slightly less conservative in their price forecasts, but the general view of the company is similar – the consensus expects a price of $166.33 with an upside of about 29.44%.

Key risks

The main risk of Hess is dependence on the external price environment. Even with low cost and hedging, if oil suddenly falls back to $50 per Brent (as it already did in 2020), cash flow immediately drops, margins fall, and investments have to be sharply slowed down. This is especially acute in the Bakken shale, where any additional duty on steel or equipment (as in 2025 at Trump's initiative) dramatically increases the capex and makes drilling less marginal. Plus, there is legal uncertainty: arbitration with Exxon and CNOOC is a story that directly affects the timing and fate of the deal with Chevron. Add to this the geopolitics of tension in regions such as Guyana or Southeast Asia, as well as the risk of regulatory changes, especially in terms of climate.

Key growth drivers

Hess has a fairly good position: on the one hand, it has a mature, reliable Bakken, on the other, Guyana, where offshore projects are already turning into a full—fledged cache generator. The sixth FPSO is already in operation, and by 2027 the volumes promise to be impressive. The merger with Chevron is a strategic step: it includes access to a lot of money, a technological upgrade, and global expertise. With oil prices in the $70-80 zone, the business will continue to generate a solid cashflow and is quite capable of pleasing shareholders with dividends and even share buybacks. And if geopolitics starts to get feverish again, and the West's demand for stable supplies increases, then Hess assets — especially in the United States and Guyana — will be shot as strategically important.

Conclusion

Hess is a balanced mining company with a clear oil focus and two powerful pillars: shale America and offshore Guyana. For investors who are looking at the horizon for several years and believe in oil, Hess is a really interesting investment idea. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $135.47 with a growth potential of 5.42% to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 6.96%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Stock Collapses, Here's When To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) (TSX:META:CA) on November 22nd, 2024 with my article “Meta Platforms: Buy This Pullback”, the stock was starting to show signs of weakness one month earlier. In the article, I argued that prior quarterly earnings performances, comparative forward valuations, and several important technical analysis signals strongly suggested that near-term losses were likely to be short-lived and that the resulting pullback marked a new buying opportunity. After publication, shares of META stock easily outperformed the broader market and generated substantial rallies of 31.47% in less than three months. At that point, it seemed as though nothing could derail the optimism for this social media stock, but bullish investors received a harsh dose of reality when META reversed sharply from the highs above $740. By the beginning of April, the stock had already lost -34.96% of its value in its descent below the $500 mark.

As bullish investors, we now face some difficult questions because the severity of the recent declines outweighs the upside trend moves that developed into the beginning of 2025. For these reasons, I think it is important to revisit some of the assumptions that were made in my previous coverage to see if any significant changes have materialized. In Meta’s fourth-quarter earnings report, I think the most glaring deficiency was seen in the company's Reality Labs segment, which posted its most substantial losses on record (-$4.97 billion). From the long-term view, this is definitely a negative growth trend that is moving in the wrong direction because this quarterly performance indicates a stark contrast when compared to the losses that were seen in the fourth-quarter of 2020 (at just -$2.1 billion). Of course, the news was not all bad because Meta’s per-share earnings figure of $8.02 surpassed consensus expectations by more than 18%. Top line figures were also quite strong at $48.39 billion, and this performance indicates annualized growth rates of 21%. Even more impressive was Meta’s net income for the period, which came in at $20.8 billion, and this indicates an annualized growth rate of 49%. So while I did have some concerns about the losses seen in Meta’s virtual reality segment, I think it is fair to say that the company managed to make up for this weakness in other areas.

In terms of the stock’s forward price-earnings metrics, META is currently trading back near levels that were previously seen in July 2023, and it should still be relatively clear that most of the Mag-7 cohort is trading at significantly higher valuations. For example, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) are currently trading with forward price-earnings ratios of 29.49x and 29.25x, respectively. Recently, metrics that have been seen with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) have become much more reasonable (at 24.5x) and a similar story can be seen with Apple Inc. (AAPL) at 27.29x. But the trends here are very clear, and we can see that only one stock in the Mag-7 peer grouping is trading at cheaper valuations right now. Of course, this would be Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) because recent declines in share prices have sent that stock toward a forward price-earnings metric of just 17.77x.

If we view these companies based on their forward price-sales metrics, Meta is in a similar but slightly worse position at 7.348x because AAPL is actually slightly cheaper at 7.297x. Interestingly, it also should probably be pointed out that Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) looks far more attractive when viewing the Mag-7 companies from this perspective because the stock is currently trading with a forward price-sales ratio of just 7.567x. Of course, GOOG still remains in a highly favorable position (at 4.992x) but AMZN is quite strong in this area (at just 2.812x). Overall, Meta Platforms is still the third-cheapest stock in the group, but there are other names in this peer group that are not far behind.

Next, we really do need to look at the price charts themselves because a great deal of destruction has been done to very important long-term price zones in this latest market crash. Without reaching too heavily into the geopolitics that surround all the various tariff scenarios causing these major declines, I would like to point out that the dominant uptrend that has been in place since the end of last year has clearly reached a point of completion. Specifically, this is the trend that accelerated with the break above resistance at $632.34 in late January, and this recent uptrend invalidation occurred as share prices were also falling below all of the major exponential moving averages on the shorter-term timeframes.

With these declines, the stock entirely defined its most recent bearish trending move with the February 14th, 2025 highs of $740.91 and the April 7th lows of $481.90. Fortunately, this allows us to use Fibonacci analysis to do a retracement study and identify important resistance levels that will need to break in order to begin re-establishing Meta’s multi-year uptrend. In the chart below, we can see that the first major resistance zone can be found at the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement (which is located near $581.10). What is concerning here is the fact that even though the stock did manage to break above this level on April 9th (with a daily close above this demarcation line), share prices quickly encountered a violent reversal in the downside direction. Of course, a lot of this erratic price action can be directly attributed to the significant market uncertainties that have characterized recent trade war headlines. But this clearly established region of resistance now gives us our pivot point because a new break above $581.10 would strongly suggest that the prior downtrend has reached a point of completion.

Finally, we will look at the stock from a longer-term perspective because there are some interesting developments that are visible on the daily timeframe. In the chart below, we can see that META shares have already fallen far below the -2 standard deviation. Of course, this is significant because we are not looking at a short-term timeframe and only 2.5% of all price action would be expected to be contained below these regions. Moreover, indicator readings in the moving average convergence divergence (MACD) are beginning to roll out of oversold territory. In terms of Elliott Wave analysis, upside projections actually appear to be quite sizable at this stage. After creating a fourth wave trough below $485, the probability of a 1.618 extension starts to increase and this would actually project an elevated price movement above $830. Personally, I do not expect to see that type of rally any time soon (given the heightened uncertainties created by various geopolitical conflicts) but this might give a rough idea of the types of gains that might be possible once the next bull market cycle develops in the broader stock benchmarks.

Ultimately, I feel as though I must reiterate the extensive headwinds that still exist for stock markets around the world. In my view, the main (and most substantial) risk for new long trades in META revolves around the fact that tremendous global trade uncertainties might weigh heavily on consumer spending and negatively impact ad revenue generated by large social media platforms (such as Facebook and Instagram). Unfortunately, this means that not even Meta Platforms will be shielded from a worst-case scenario trade war outcome. Additionally, the company’s widening losses in the virtual reality segment could be an issue that lead to negative surprises in the upcoming first-quarter earnings report. But now that we have already seen the stock break below several important price levels (including the -2 standard deviation on the daily timeframe), I think that further downside might still be relatively limited when compared to most stocks in the Mag-7 peer group. As a result, I will be raising my outlook to a “strong buy” rating because I think that the share price declines that we have seen over the last few months have created a great opportunity to buy the stock while it’s still on sale. For all of these reasons, I think that the current price levels seen right here should be considered for potential long entries, but position sizes should be kept smaller than usual given the severity of the recent turmoil in global markets.

data.title: A World In Flux: Managing Evolving Risks In Equity Markets
data.description:
Originally published on March 27, 2025

By Nelson Yu & Jonathan Berkow

Risk management is being put to the test in 2025. How can equity portfolio teams cope with multiple hazards across equity markets this year?

It's hard to keep up with this year's fast-changing risk landscape. From artificial intelligence to tariffs to macroeconomic uncertainty, threats are mounting to businesses, equity holdings and portfolio returns. Bottom-up portfolio managers focused on company-specific outcomes could be particularly vulnerable to rapidly changing dynamics. This, in our view, is the most important overarching risk to manage in the current environment.

Put differently, our task is to ensure that portfolio returns are driven by long-term company outcomes and not the latest policy change. To this end, we recommend a "mosaic approach" to risk management, using different tools to address different risks. In the following case studies, we demonstrate how equity investors can apply these tools strategically to mitigate today's evolving risks and to keep portfolios aligned with their long-term strategic objectives.

AI and Energy: Cluster Analysis Uncovers Correlation

Rapid advances in AI continue to create investor enthusiasm-and uncertainty. In 2024, investors began paying closer attention to AI's energy demands. That storyline became more prominent in early 2025 after DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) unveiled a more efficient AI model that could be run with lower energy intensity. As a result, in late January and February, AI-related technology stocks sold off along with energy stocks that were considered vulnerable to lower AI-driven demand, including nuclear power generators.

Traditional risk management techniques struggle to identify correlations between sectors or industries that don't seem to have much in common. Portfolio managers (PMs) monitor various prevalent risks, including country and sector risks, to ensure balanced allocations. However, static labels like countries and sectors often fail to reflect firms' evolving business models and regional shifts, necessitating more frequent updates. This is increasingly important as new trends, technologies and economic opportunities emerge. Cluster risk analysis helps PMs dynamically examine and manage risks by identifying links between stocks based on their movements.

Like most investors, we didn't predict the market's reaction to DeepSeek's breakthrough. However, we have used cluster analysis to identify correlated performance patterns of AI-technology stocks and some energy stocks-two groups that wouldn't intuitively seem related. Traditionally, energy stocks have tended to be more correlated with oil prices and underlying economic growth, while AI stocks have been the major force driving equity markets higher over the last two years.

Cluster analysis looks for correlated sources of risk that aren't obvious to traditional quantitative risk models or fundamental analysis. It's based on sophisticated machine-learning techniques that classify stocks into groups whose returns have been moving closely together over a defined period.

In this case, some energy and utility stocks were placed in the same cluster as AI-related stocks. This indicates that energy stocks are aligned with AI, reflecting the energy consumption of training models. Understanding this dynamic helps us assess more completely the level of a portfolio's exposure to the AI theme.

Tariffs: Untangling a Web of Consequences

Since President Donald Trump took office in January, tariff talk has dominated headlines. Tariffs create cost and pricing uncertainties that affect companies in complex ways. For investment teams, tariffs are a big headache: how can you gauge the exposure of holdings to this risk and the potential impact on earnings and profitability, especially when we don't know which tariffs will stick?

Analysts could study the supply chains of every stock covered to manually evaluate which of a product's input components-often in the hundreds-are subject to tariffs. This would be painstaking and inefficient.

Instead, tools like broker baskets can improve our ability to quantify the tariff shock's impact on an entire portfolio and identify highly exposed positions. Sector insights can help us pinpoint bigger potential winners and losers, enabling us to better calibrate risks and position sizes.

Broker baskets are groups of stocks that brokers believe are likely to rise or fall based on certain outcomes, such as higher tariffs (Display). However, representative broker baskets have blind spots since they include a limited number of names. So our portfolio teams start with broker baskets but extend the analysis to every stock in a relevant investment universe to understand how they covary with tariff risk.

Broker Baskets: Gauging Tariff Risk for Stocks and Portfolios

This exercise helps surface potential winners and losers. For example, companies with more local production and domestic operations might benefit during a trade war. Conversely, major retailers that rely on imported goods would be more vulnerable to tariffs on their overseas suppliers.

Sector sensitivities are also instructive. Technology and materials are among the sectors that are more likely to suffer from tariffs, while utilities and financials might benefit (Display). Still, there's a range of dispersion within each sector, reflecting differences in companies and business models.

Sensitivities to Tariffs Vary Between and Within Sectors

Some of these observations might sound obvious for fundamental analysts who cover individual stocks. But in fast-moving markets, information on all holdings can't always be updated in real time. The techniques above allow us to identify companies at risk and aggregate exposures to the portfolio level, providing a portfolio manager with a real-time window into an allocation's susceptibility to tariff risks.

Identifying positions at risk is just a first step. The next step is to drill down into the holdings surfaced by the broker baskets and to follow up with fundamental research.

Research analysts with deep sector or industry expertise can confirm or reject the quantitative recommendations. What's more, this analysis can help an investment firm with multiple portfolios check positioning across the platform, ensuring a balance of overweight/underweight positions in companies on both sides of the tariff-risk equation. Beyond tariffs, broker baskets can help manage other policy risks, including healthcare, tax policy and defense.

Inflation: Monitoring Top-Down Risk in Bottom-Up Portfolios

Macroeconomic risks are ever present in financial markets. Even for investment teams rooted in bottom-up stockpicking based on company fundamentals, the impact of top-down macro variables and the effects of the economic cycle on business results cannot be ignored.

Interest rates, yield curves, commodity prices, wages and inflation are among the well-known and widely observed macro risks. To aid fundamental analysts, we can monitor these risks from the top down. Drawing on daily measures of each macro variable, our quantitative experts can estimate how stocks move with them. Just as every stock has a beta to the market-which gauges its correlation to market moves-each stock also has measurable sensitivities to macro factors like inflation.

Stocks within an industry may have different sensitivities to inflation. For example, Constellation Energy (CEG) exhibits positive correlation with inflation, possibly because of its nuclear energy business, which may become more attractive if inflation rises (Display). Exxon Mobil's (XOM) slightly positive inflation beta could indicate its ability to pass on higher prices to consumers, in our view. In contrast, Vestas Wind Systems (OTCPK:VWSYF), a Danish company, suffers when inflation expectations rise, perhaps because rising prices in its supply chains would raise implementation costs for wind farms and erode profitability.

Inflation Betas Provide Intelligence on Stock Sensitivities

In the retail sector, companies such as Costco (COST) and Kroger (KR) in the US and Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDF) in the UK show similar inflation betas, slightly above and below neutral. In our view, this implies that big brands benefiting from customer loyalty are more capable of passing on higher prices to consumers without taking a hit to earnings.

Inflation betas change over time and require interpretation by fundamental analysts. After identifying and interpreting the results, we aim to aggregate inflation sensitivities to the portfolio and review them weekly. Portfolio exposures can change when stocks with high or low sensitivities are bought or sold, or when companies make strategic adjustments to curb the effects of inflation.

Combining Quantitative Tools and Fundamental Expertise

What do the above risk scenarios have in common? As we see it, each issue and risk management approach requires a strategic combination of quantitative tools and fundamental research. Quantitative, data-driven tools provide portfolio teams with robust, real-time information that can surface risks across vast investment universes. Deep industry expertise is needed to interpret the data and reach meaningful fundamental conclusions about individual company exposures.

After using the tools described above to uncover portfolio risks, the investment team must then decide whether it has an edge in forecasting the outcome of that risk and size positions appropriately. This process can also unlock opportunities; when a stock is mistakenly associated with a risk, it might sell off more than it deserves and present an attractive buying opportunity.

Applying advanced risk management techniques is essential to investing with conviction in today's uncertain environment, which helps clients stay invested through short-term volatility and remain focused on long-term investing plans. Combining quantitative data science and fundamental analysis is the best way, in our view, to ensure that long-term company outcomes remain at the heart of an investing process designed to meet clients' strategic financial objectives.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

data.title: Yields Up To 10%: REITs To Buy Right Now
data.description:
For the past few years, REIT (VNQ) share prices have crashed even as most REITs kept hiking their dividend payments:

As a result, REIT dividend yields are today historically high.

It is common now to find even high-quality REITs offering relatively safe and sustainable dividend yields in the 8-10% range.

Here are three examples that we are buying:

Healthcare Realty (HR)

Healthcare Realty Trust Incorporated is a leading REIT focusing on medical office buildings.

This is one of the best-performing property sectors in today's market.

It enjoys growing demand from the rapidly aging population. Yet, it is limited in supply, and we have not built enough of them in recent years due to the surge in interest rates and high construction costs.

This has resulted in growing occupancy rates and strong rent growth:

This year, again, HR has guided for the growth to continue. It expects its same property NOI to rise another 3%, and this growth could even accelerate in the coming years. Here is what their CEO said on their most recent earnings call:

"Medical office building market fundamentals are strong with demand for outpatient space outstripping supply. We are benefiting from the secular tailwinds of aging demographics and the shift in care to outpatient settings. Taking advantage of this backdrop, I'm proud of our leasing team for producing their fifth consecutive quarter of over 400,000 square feet of new signed leases in the multi-tenant portfolio.

I'm also pleased to report our team delivered another quarter of strong multi-tenant absorption, totaling 159,000 square feet or 49 basis points. This occupancy gain was driven by 565,000 square feet of new lease commencements, coupled with strong tenant retention of over 80%. This is tremendous execution by all members of our team. We've gained 164 basis points of occupancy over the last four quarters. With one quarter to go in our published five quarter occupancy bridge, we are on pace to be at the high end of our 150 to 200 basis point goal.

NOI growth was also solid in the third quarter. We achieved same-store property year-over-year growth of 3.1%... We expect NOI growth to accelerate as timing differences moderate and free rent burns off...

Our core business is performing extremely well. This performance is generating a number of tailwinds into 2025... [emphasis added]

But despite this strong performance, the company's stock price has crashed with the rest of the REIT sector, and it is today heavily discounted, trading at just 10.5x FFO (funds from operations), an estimated 30% discount to net asset value, and offering an 8% dividend yield:

Why is it so cheap then?

Is it overleveraged? No. The company has an investment-grade-rated balance sheet with a reasonable 38% LTV and well-staggered debt maturities.

Is it poorly managed? No. Management is focused on growing on a per-share basis, and has proved its alignment with shareholders by selling many assets in recent years to buy back shares at a discount.

Rather, we think that HR is unfairly beaten down for two reasons.

The first reason is that investors still associate medical office buildings with the office sector. They fear that vacant office buildings could be converted into medical office buildings, leading to a surge in new supply. But if this was a real risk, why hasn't it happened yet? The pandemic started over 5 years ago. In reality, this is not possible in most cases. To quote Nuveen:

From a physical standpoint, medical outpatient buildings require a substantially different buildout, including increased plumbing, higher floor load capacity, ample parking ratios, differentiated HVAC/electrical loads, biohazardous waste removal, and different floorplan.

The second reason is that there is significant uncertainty about the future direction of the company. A major activist investor called Starboard Value recently took a major stake in the company, and it is pushing for changes. The CEO, CFO, and a few board members have resigned and are now being replaced.

Starboard is looking for ways to unlock value, which is great, but the market does not like this type of uncertainty. Income investors are especially concerned as they worry that this could lead to a new capital allocation strategy and a dividend cut.

The previous CEO maintained that the dividend was sustainable, but perhaps the new CEO could decide to retain more cash flow for growth.

I am fine either way, and expect strong long-term returns as the company regains a more reasonable valuation. However, I understand that this would make many investors more hesitant.

Our estimate of fair value is around 16x FFO. To achieve this, the stock would need to rise by 50%. Lower interest rates, further asset sales, and buybacks will be the catalysts.

We think that the risk-to-reward is particularly attractive coming from a recession-resistant REIT.

Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR)

Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc.'s biggest tenant, PharmaCann, failed to pay its rent in full in December.

It is a very large tenant, representing 16.5% of IIPR's rental income, so naturally, this concerns the market.

It has caused IIPR's share price to crash by 47% following the news:

We have no interest in the company's common equity, especially not for our Retirement Portfolio. The cannabis property sector is just too risky.

However, the company also has some preferred equity, and we find it very interesting because it dropped as well despite offering a significant margin of safety.

It now offers a 10% dividend yield, and we think that it is ideal for our retirement portfolio, which seeks to maximize safe income.

What's unique about IIPR is that it has very little debt.

It has only $300 million of debt, representing a very low 11% LTV. The company enjoys a 17x debt service ratio.

It is so low that the company could repay almost all of its debt in just one year if it stopped paying its common dividend and used its cash flow for deleveraging. It also has $130 million in cash and equivalent right now and an additional $87.5 million in liquidity via its credit facility.

Therefore, we think that bankruptcy is extremely unlikely in the case of IIPR.

Yes, this is a risky property sector, and IIPR is facing major tenant issues, but it is very hard to go belly-up when there is very little debt.

Even if IIPR faced a cascade of tenant bankruptcies and had to release its properties at materially lower rent levels, the debt could still be easily serviced, and the preferred equity would still enjoy significant coverage.

This is exactly what you want when you invest in the preferred equity of a REIT. You want the REIT to have low debt and the preferred equity to represent only a small slice so that it enjoys a big common equity buffer.

If this was any other property sector, the dividend yield on this preferred equity would be closer to 6% with such a strong balance sheet.

Yet, it is 10% today.

A certain yield spread is, of course, warranted, but we think that it is more than appropriate and will result in alpha over the long run.

As a reminder, IIPR is generally a high-quality REIT. They own a well-diversified portfolio of mostly cannabis cultivation facilities in limited license jurisdictions. This means that its properties enjoy a moat in their local markets by means of their license and also their replacement cost. The demand for cannabis is rising, but the supply of such facilities is limited, and therefore, their value should be sustainable.

Cannabis companies are, of course, risky businesses, and lease defaults are a regular occurrence for these landlords, but historically, IIPR has been able to release properties and/or work out solutions with existing tenants with limited losses. As an example, IIPR faced significant tenant issues in 2022, representing about 15% of its rental income, but it managed to recover most of it within one year by releasing and/or selling assets.

We find that IIPR has done a good job at mitigating risks the best it can, but it still recognizes that this is a riskier sector, and this is why they maintain a fortress balance sheet.

The management also has one of the best track records of the entire REIT sector, having managed to grow its FFO and dividend per share at a rapid pace and significantly outperformed the broader REIT market:

Today, it is facing issues, its cash flow is likely to decrease in 2025, and its common dividend may even get cut, but the preferred dividend enjoys significant coverage, and bankruptcy seems extremely unlikely to us given what we previously explained.

For this reason, we are happy to buy the dip and have now started building a position in the now 10% yielding Series A preferred shares of IIPR.

Closing Note

REITs are a great place to hunt for high-yielding opportunities right now. Most of them are heavily discounted, despite continuing to grow their dividend payments year after year.

data.title: Consolidated Edison Keeps The Lights On While Investors Sleep Well At Night
data.description:
Investing Thesis

Over the past few weeks, I have spent some time looking at a variety of defensive investment possibilities in an effort to add a modicum of stability to my portfolio as the markets continue to experience turmoil in the wake of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff announcements. As I have discussed in my recent articles on FirstEnergy (FE) and Alliant Energy Corporation (LNT), I tend to look towards utilities stocks whenever I am particularly spooked by roiling economic waters because I know that most Americans will continue to turn on their lights, take hot showers, run their air conditioners, and cook on their gas stoves no matter what. As someone who grew up in the greater New York City area, I have long known Consolidated Edison (NYSE:ED) as the utility in the city and, while I have never bought any shares in the company, it has been on my radar for quite a long time. As a government-sanctioned legal monopoly in the nation's most densely-populated region, I am drawn to the prospects of owning shares in a company with revenue that is highly regulated and rate-based, with millions of customers locked into its services. After all, such companies tend to provide investors with a predictable, growing, and recession-resistant flow of income. In this article, I will evaluate Consolidated Edison from the perspective of an income investor with an eye on determining whether or not I should add it to my portfolio.

Company Profile

Providing electric, gas, and steam services to more than 10 million customers concentrated in the New York Metropolitan area, Consolidated Edison is one of the largest publicly-traded energy companies in the United States. The company's operations consist primarily of three subsidiaries: the eponymous Consolidated Edison Company of New York (CECONY), which serves all five boroughs of New York City and Westchester County; Orange and Rockland Utilities (O&R), which serves southeastern New York State and parts of northern New Jersey; and Con Edison Transmission, Inc, which invests in high-voltage electric transmission and gas pipeline endeavors in partnership with other utility providers around the northeastern U.S.

Financial Performance

In the company's most recent earnings report, released in late February, Consolidated Edison reported a 4.04% increase in revenues to $15.26 billion in 2024, but net earnings experienced a 27.75% decline to $1.82 billion. While the company's earnings fell Consolidated Edison nevertheless managed to maintain its full-year adjusted earnings guidance of between $5.20 and $5.40 per share, landing at the upper end of that range:

Still, it is worth looking into the decreased EPS to assess whether or not it indicates an underlying concern with the company. As we can see here, Consolidated Edison reported a net income of $310 million (or $0.90 per share in the graphic above) for the fourth quarter of 2024 compared with $335 million of ($0.97 per share) in the fourth quarter of 2023. The company also reported an adjusted earnings of $340 million (or $0.98 per share) for the quarter ending in December, marking a modest decline from $346 million (or $1.00 per share) during the same period in 2023. Consolidated Edison also reported a fairly substantial drop in net income for the full year, revealing a decrease from $2.519 billion (or $7.25 per share in the graphic above) in 2023 to $1.82 billion (or $5.26 per share) in 2024. The company's adjusted earnings for the full year, however, showed a modest climb from $1.762 (or $5.07 per share) billion in 2023 to $1.868 billion (or $5.40 per share) in 2024.

Consolidated Edison's decline in net income can be attributed to a lack of gains accompanying the sale of the company's clean energy businesses at the tail end of 2023. The growth in adjusted earnings reflects continued strength in Consolidated Edison's utility rate plans and the company's infrastructure investments.

Dividend

When Consolidated Edison hiked its quarterly dividend by $0.02 per share in January, the company marked its 51st consecutive annual dividend hike, extending a streak it began during the Gerald Ford administration. Not surprisingly, Consolidated Edison prides itself on its status as a Dividend Aristocrat and King, highlighting this fact in its annual report:

Indeed, Consolidated Edison's half-century of annual dividend hikes is the longest of any utility in the S&P 500. Taking a look at Seeking Alpha's quant grades for Consolidated Edison suggests that the company's dividend is a safe, if somewhat, modest one when compared with its peers in the utility sector:

As might be expected with a highly regulated, mature company, Consolidated Edison offers neither the juiciest yield (3.15%) nor the most mouth-watering of recent dividend growth rates (a CAGR of 2.27%), but I think Seeking Alpha's quant score for the stock's yield is somewhat misleading. Consolidated Edison's yield of 3.15%, despite the disappointing D+ we see here, nevertheless exceeds that of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU)'s 2.98%. In other words, Consolidated Edison offers exactly what one would hope for from a Dividend Aristocrat in the utilities sector. With a payout ration just north of 60%, Consolidated Edison's yield is reasonably high, well-covered by earnings, boasts a long track record of growth, and it's continued growth trajectory is a stated priority of the company's management. When looking at a legal monopoly such as Consolidated Edison, we are not seeking eye-bulging growth of principal or hand-over-fist growth in dividends. Instead, we are looking for a low-beta investment with predictable payouts to provide anchor-like stability to our portfolios. Consolidated Edison fits the bill.

Future Growth Prospects

As I discuss in the previous section, Consolidated Edison's dividend payout ratio sits squarely in the middle of the target range of 55% to 65% of adjusted earnings set by the company's management. With a conservatively-managed dividend policy, Consolidated Edison does not sacrifice the funding of capital investments to pay shareholders a slightly higher-than-average yield.

Looking towards the next few years, the company anticipates an average annual growth in earnings per share of between 6% and 7% through the 2028 fiscal year, driven largely by growth in customer base rates as well as capital investments in clean energy infrastructure. Consolidated Edison has plans to invest $5,122 million in 2025, $8,067 million in 2026, and upwards of $24,469 million in aggregate from 2027 to 2029. Some of the company's investments include:

  • The development of smart grid technologies

One of the company's most important infrastructure investments can be found in Consolidated Edison's continued development of the smart grid. By leveraging cutting-edge digital resources, smart grid technology saves consumers money by providing insight into electric usage habits and highlighting energy-saving strategies based on individual user behavior.

  • The development of offshore wind generation

Consolidated Edison has plans to invest in The Brooklyn Clean Energy Hub, an $810 million transmission substation that will be built on the former site of gas turbines adjacent to the Vinegar Hill neighborhood near the East River. The Hub will help bring offshore wind and other clean energy to the power grid in New York City.

  • Investing in three Reliable Clean Cities Projects

Consolidated Edison is investing close to $800 million to building new substations and power lines in Queens, Staten Island, and Brooklyn to bring electricity produced by wind, solar, and hydroelectric plants in New York State to customers in New York City.

  • Expanding EV charging infrastructure

In a partnership with the City of New York and FLO, Consolidated Edison aims to create an extensive network of EV charging stations to support customers without home charging, thereby enabling more New Yorkers to make the switch to electric vehicles.

With cheaper, more efficient clean energy, newer power substations, and smart grid technologies in the works, Consolidated Edison looks positioned for continued growth while the highly regulated nature of the utility

Concluding Thoughts

While Consolidated Edison may not be the fastest-growing or highest-yielding utility stock in the market, it is one of the most consistent. With a dividend growth streak old enough to join AARP and a massive customer base, it is easy to see why so many investors consider the company to be a cornerstone of their portfolios. Thanks to its status as a legal monopoly, its investments in modern and future-looking infrastructure, and its predictable cash flows, Consolidated Edison should be on any income investor's short list. I consider it to be a "buy." What do you think? I'd love to hear your thoughts on the company in the comments!

data.title: NRO: Struggling To Grow In High Interest Rate Environment
data.description:
Overview

Neuberger Berman Real Estate Securities Income Fund (NYSE:NRO) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of diverse real estate investments. As we remain in a higher interest rate environment, there are plenty of high quality REITs that remain at a discounted valuation. However, it can be difficult to accumulate a position in multiple opportunities at the same time. So I've been looking for a solid real estate focused fund that maintains exposure to high quality holdings and is able to provide a mix of both income and capital appreciation.

Looking at the performance of NRO over the last twelve months, the price has decreased by about 6.3%. However, most of that decline happened over the last two week period as a result of the market volatility driven by President Trump's ongoing tariff proposals. When including all distributions that have been paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at about 3.2% over the same time frame. NRO has a generous starting dividend yield of 12.5%, which makes it an attractive choice for investors looking to create a sizeable supplemental income stream within their portfolio. However, I worry about the sustainability of the distribution going forward.

NRO has a public inception dating back to 2003, meaning we have over two decades worth of performance data to reference. The most updated reporting seems to indicate that the fund has trouble displaying any meaningful NAV growth. While growth has trended in a positive direction over the last fiscal year, the interest rate environment and market volatility still make it difficult for the fund to succeed in the near future. Additionally, it seems like NRO has had trouble generating a sufficient amount of earnings to fully support the annual distributions. So let's start by taking a look at NRO's underlying strategy and investment approach.

Fund Strategy

According to the most updated fact sheet, NRO has total assets of $224M that is spread across approximately 81 different holdings. The fund uses a blended approach by having a nearly 67% exposure to common stocks and an approximate 33% exposure to preferred stocks. From a sector perspective, Health Care investments account for the largest portion of NRO's portfolio at 12.1%. This is followed by exposure to Telecommunications and Data Center REITs, accounting for 11.6% and 10.6% respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up about 34.3% of the total portfolio so we can assume that these sectors will have the greatest impact on NRO's daily price change and total return potential.

I do like the Data center exposure that NRO provides, considering the projected growth to take place throughout the AI sector. As the demand and utility around Artificial Intelligence grows, so will the necessary spending on Data centers. For instance, McKinsey has a midrange estimate that sees the market share of Data centers increasing at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 22% through 2030. As we can see, NRO's top position is currently Equinix (EQIX) at 8.3% of assets. EQIX currently has a strong buy rating by Wall Street, with an average price target of $1,018 per share which indicates a potential upside of 31%.

NRO also has exposure to other high quality real estate investments like Prologis (PLD) and American Tower Corp. (AMT), accounting for 8.1% and 6.9% of assets respectively. I do like the mixed focus here as NRO maintains a diverse exposure to different industries, which can let the fund partake in sector specific bull markets. Here are some notable achievements of a few of the top holdings:

  • Welltower (WELL): saw its share price increase by over 170% over the last five years. WELL is set to capitalize on the growing demand for senior housing.
  • Sun Communities (SUI): has very conservative dividend payout ratio of approximately 58%.
  • Public Storage (PSA): has a higher dividend yield of 4.3% and has been able to increase its dividend at a CAGR of 8.45% over the last five years.

As part of the fund's strategy, NRO utilizes some leverage in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. Leverage currently amounts to 23.7% of assets, which I would like to see reduced in this higher interest rate environment. Higher interest rates directly translate to a higher cost of borrowing, which can make it more difficult for NRO to grow its earnings. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage could be used on an investment that doesn't work out as planned, which will amplify losses.

Valuation

Since NRO operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, the price now trades at a slight discount to NAV of about 5.4%. For reference, NRO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.4% over the last three years, so I would consider the price to actually be trading around its fair value. Looking back over the last ten years below, we can see that NRO's price to NAV relationship is constantly shifting and I believe this can be attributed to the fund's ability to generate earnings.

The most recent annual report is dated as of October 2024 and reveals a few key things. Net investment income landed at $0.12 per share while net realized gains amounted to $1.16 per share for 2024. This resulted in a total growth from investment operations of $1.28 per share, which was a strong year of growth for NRO. However, the fund has failed to deliver meaningful NAV growth over the last five years due to the fund's reliance on net realized gains.

Net investment income has never exceeded $0.13 per share, which is a bit odd for a fund full of income producing REITs. It's also weird considering that a sizeable portion of the portfolio is allocated towards preferred securities that are more likely to pay out consistent income. As a result, NRO's growth is dependent on positive market conditions that can lead to net realized gains. In the future, I would love to see NRO's underlying performance improve by growing net investment income. This would create a more stable price and make it more likely for NRO to successfully navigate challenging macro conditions. For instance, here are the NAV amounts at each year end.

  • 2020: $3.89
  • 2021: $5.25
  • 2022: $3.40
  • 2023: $2.99
  • 2024: $3.90

To NRO's credit, the real estate sector has continued to suffer through headwinds ever since Covid happened in 2020. Covid drove markets down and then the Fed rapidly hiked interest rates shortly after, which caused the sector to decline. We haven't really been able to navigate through a 'normal' market over the last few years and real estate hasn't really had the chance to normalize. Hopefully the Fed will implement interest rate cuts, which should serve as a catalyst for growth in the real estate sector.

Risk Profile

The biggest risk will continue to be the current interest rate environment. Interest rates were aggressively hiked throughout 2022 and 2023 in response to record breaking inflationary data. Ever since then, the price has remained below its prior highs. A higher interest rate directly correlates to a higher cost of borrowing, as well as increased interest expenses that the fund has to pay out on its leverage. Since the real estate sector is heavily reliant on access to affordable debt capital, growth tends to slow in a higher interest rate environment. Since it becomes more costly to pursue growth initiatives like acquisitions, new developments, or marketing campaigns, businesses typically shift to focus on capital efficiency instead.

Looking at the price chart below, we can see how NRO's price has shared an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate. When interests rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic, NRO's price rapidly increased over the year. Therefore, I anticipate that future interest rate cuts will serve as a catalyst for growth.

However, the risk remains that we are likely to stay in a higher interest rate environment over the next twelve months, which can keep NRO's price suppressed. Inflation rates remain higher than the Fed anticipates and they've shifted their outlook to a more hawkish stance. At the end of 2024, the Fed estimated four interest rate cuts to take place over 2025, but this has now been reduced to only one estimated interest rate cut. If interest rates were to increase, we've likely see NRO's price go lower.

Dividend

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0312 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.5%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.3744 per share. Looking back over the dividend history, we can see that the payouts have remained flat since 2021. NRO also has a history of decreases in the past, but I don't actually think that the dividend is too large. I think the decreases ultimately come from the fund's flawed strategy. The fund needs to improve the net investment income so that it can offset losses on the sale of positions. For instance, NRO only covered the distribution two out of the last five years. Here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: ($1.51)
  • 2021: $1.75
  • 2022: ($1.48)
  • 2023: ($0.04)
  • 2024: $1.28

The annual report indicates that the majority of the distributions are coming from a combination of net investment income and return of capital. This could indicate that the portions of the dividend will be classified as ordinary dividend income. Conversely, return of capital distributions aren't taxed directly. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are deferred until the time of sale. Based on your specific circumstance, a position in NRO may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate NRO as a hold due to the inconsistent NAV growth and weak internal earnings. The latest annual report reveals that the fund's net investment income has historically been weak. Net investment losses have weighed down any NAV momentum. The distribution amount doesn't seem to be an issue but the macroeconomic conditions still remain unfavorable. Higher interest rates can suppress valuations and make it a lot harder for NRO to grow.

data.title: Regions Financial: Capital Is A Focus Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
Shares of Regions Financial (NYSE:RF) have been hard hit during the recent market sell-off and have now essentially erased all of their gains over the past year with shares roughly 30% off their highs. With the stock set to report Q1 earnings on Thursday April 17th, this is a critical opportunity for the company to stabilize sentiment. I had been cautious on RF for some time, but upgraded shares to a “hold” in December. In hindsight, I should have just remained a sell, given shares have lost about 26%. In this article, I will preview Q1 results and whether investors should take a position into the release.

Recent growth has been sluggish despite a favorable geography

Regions Financial is among the larger regional banks in the country, and it primarily operates in the Southeast or Sun Belt region of the United States. RF is committed to growing further, primarily by deepening its exposure to key markets rather than expanding outside of core markets. Given the relatively fragmented state of the industry, this makes sense as there remains plenty of opportunity for RF to grow and scale inside its key markets. In particular, it is focused on Texas and Florida as growth drivers.

Before previewing results, it is worth reviewing recent results. In Q4, Regions earned $0.56, bringing full year earnings to $1.93. For the quarter as well as the year, the story was one of really modest growth, despite these ambitions. As you can see below, its deposits were essentially flat from a year ago. This meets my criteria of “stable” deposits, but RF has not really been growing.

Over time, we do need to see a bank grow its deposits in order to increase its loan book, drive net interest income (NII) and increase its EPS. One other notable factor is that its cost of deposits rose 6bps year over year, even as the Fed funds rate was 100bps lower. This reflects the fact that it had faced some outflows in noninterest-bearing (NIB) deposits, which now appear to be stabilizing, as well as repriced maturing time deposits at higher yields.

Because of higher funding costs, its 355bp net interest margin (NIM) while up 1bp sequentially was down 5bps from a year earlier. In Q1, we should see some modest benefit from a full quarter of the December rate cut. Its interest-bearing deposits exited Q4 at a 2.07% cost, down from a 2.13% average in Q4.

Given weak deposit growth, RF had $96.4 billion of loans, down $600 million sequentially and 2% from a year earlier. In particular, business loans were lower, and the company is gradually reducing its commercial real estate exposure (CRE). As with most banks, RF is also seeing a weakening of credit quality with nonperforming loans (NPL) rising. Pre-pandemic, its NPL ratio averaged 1.07%, so there may be some more weakening here. Charge-offs were 49bps, at the high-end of its 40-50bp long-term target.

Lack of growth likely to limit Q1 and full year EPS

Looking ahead, analysts are currently expecting the company to earn $0.51 in Q1. The company has beaten estimates in just five of the past ten quarters. Generally speaking, I like to see companies beat estimates at least 70% of the time, and this history is consistent with my view of RF being a mixed operator. Now, there are two fewer calendar days in Q1. This should be a $0.03 headwind to earnings all else equal, so if RF performed exactly in line with Q4, we would see about $0.53 of EPS.

There are several other factors to consider. First on its average balance sheet, I expect to see flattish to slightly down deposits. Typically, municipalities begin drawing dawn deposits after receiving property tax revenue in Q4; partially offsetting this, consumer balances benefit from tax refunds. RF is expecting some deposit growth after Q1, but I remain cautious on its ability to grow deposits after a fairly difficult time doing so over the past two years. I will also be focused on if RF was able to reduce the cost of deposits further.--I expect just modest improvement

On the loan side, management had guided that the loan pipeline is trending up and it expected H2 2025 growth. I am very cautious on the potential for loan growth. With so much macro uncertainty and rates staying higher, I do not expect businesses to engage in as much discretionary cap-ex, which will likely reduce loan demand longer term and challenge full year guidance.

Credit losses are likely a post-Q1 risk

On credit quality, the question for the sector is how much they react to tariffs and potential economics downside. In its first quarter, Wells Fargo (WFC) took $932 million in provisions, similar to last year’ $938 million and down from $1.1 billion in Q4. This is the big bank that most resembles regionals. I expect RF and others to similarly take a wait and see approach.

We are more likely to see large reserve builds in Q2 or Q3 if the economy does indeed turn down rather than in Q1, I expect. RF has $1.73 billion of allowances. I would rather see this above $1.85 billion or 2x NPLs, and so I expect to see some gradual builds. In particular, RF has $15 billion of CRE. It has just 10% exposure in office vs 29% in apartments and 7% to homebuilders.

With Sun Belt real estate valuations struggling, I could see some reserve builds in its apartment exposure, though I expect minimal losses. Within office, it has $241 million of nonperforming loans but just $111 million in reserves. While its loans have a low 69% LTV, this is a low coverage ratio, and I expect reserves to rise in 2025.

On noninterest revenue, asset growth should boost wealth management and capital markets. However with market volatility, I expect to see capital markets activity weaken considerably and be a headwind in Q2-Q4. Similarly with rates staying elevated, mortgage income is likely to be constrained.

Low adjusted capital is the primary headwind

RF also has a $30.7 billion securities portfolio, which has $2 billion of unrealized losses. Like many banks, RF has purchased securities when yields were lower and is now carrying them at a loss. It has substantial fixed rate hedges at lower yields, as well. As these mature, we should see some NII improvement, but it will be a very slow process with limited 2025 uplift.

These losses are also important to consider when looking at its capital position. RF ended the year with a 10.8% common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio, which is quite healthy at first glance. However when we factor in unrealized losses, capital dips to 8.8%, below its target of 9.25-9.75%.

In Q1, I expect both metrics to be down 10-20bps, but in Q2 if rates rise further, we could see losses worsen. At the start of the year, RF expects its losses to decline to $1.6 billion this year, adding 34bps to capital. I now expect just half of this benefit to be realized in 2025. This means RF will take longer to bring capital to its targeted range. I expect very limited repurchases after $58 million of Q4 buybacks, though I view its 5.3% dividend as secure.

RF’s weak adjusted capital position is an increased concern in a worsening macro environment, as it has less room to manage losses. This is one reason I have preferred Key (KEY), which has taken actions to enhance its capital position and reduce the impact of unrealized losses.

In terms of guidance, I do see downside risk to its targeted loan growth, which in turn could weigh on NII guidance. I also see some upside risk to charge-offs. I do think RF will make just modest tweaks (ie 0-2%), given how much uncertainty remains.

Shares are not expensive, but upside catalysts appear unlikely

Back in December, I had seen $2.20-$2.35 in EPS in 2025. Given slower balance sheet growth and more credit losses, I now see earnings of $1.90-$2.25. I appreciate this is a wider range than 3 months ago, but there is substantially more uncertainty on the economic outlook. I would expect $0.51-$0.53 in Q1 earnings, for a modest beat. Shares are just about 10x earnings, which is not expensive. As such, I do not see a need to sell shares, especially as there is unlikely to be a serious miss in Q1, in my view.

That said, in this period of uncertainty, I believe investors are likely to place a premium on banks with strong capital positions. Downside economic tail risks have risen over the past month. In the central case, RF can retain earnings to return capital to better levels, but in a downside case, capital will constrain its balance sheet flexibility further. As such, I struggle to see an argument for multiple expansion with capital likely to be below target all year. With banks like Key also around 10x earnings, I think there are names with more upside potential in the regional sector, and I remain a hold on RF.

data.title: Genmab's Outlook Beyond Darzalex's Patent Cliff
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Solomon Oshabaheebwa as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Genmab (GMAB) presents a potentially rewarding long-term investment opportunity, though current uncertainties warrant a cautious approach. The primary concern is the pending patent expiration for Darzalex, Genmab's blockbuster drug that contributed 65% of FY24 revenue. In this analysis, I assessed how Genmab is positioned to weather this significant transition. I examined the revenue potential of Genmab’s other commercial products, its late-stage pipeline, anticipated product launches, and balance sheet strength. I then made revenue projections that model "best-case" and "worst-case" scenarios as Darzalex royalties decline. Despite the looming patent cliff, my findings suggest that Genmab's robust pipeline and strategic positioning make it a compelling low-risk, high-reward opportunity for patient investors.

Company Overview

Genmab develops customized antibodies that target markers on the surface of cancerous cells or other molecules associated with various illnesses. They make the antibodies through five proprietary technology platforms termed: DuoBody, HexaBody, DuoHexaBody, HexElect, and Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs). They have successfully licensed their technology to several Biotech behemoths, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Novartis (NVS), and Amgen (AMGN), that incorporate the antibodies in various therapies. These partnerships yield milestone revenues tied to certain development goals, and royalties on sales.

Until recently, Genmab did not participate in commercialization instead focusing on technology development. It began to expand its own commercialization capabilities with TIVDAK for cervical cancer in 2021, sharing commercial responsibilities and profits with Pfizer in various markets. Starting 2025, Genmab will have sole responsibility outside the US and China. The company also co-commercializes EPKINLY/TEPKINLY with AbbVie, leading efforts in the US and Japan while AbbVie takes charge in Europe. The company aims to expand its wholly owned portfolio in the near term with new candidates Rina-S and Acasunlimab.

DARZALEX’s dominance presents a concentration risk.

In FY2024, Genmab reported revenues of $3 billion of which 65% was generated from DARZALEX royalties. This heavy dependence on a single product poses a significant vulnerability to future revenues as patent expiration approaches. As I will discuss later, the other products on the market are unlikely to outgrow Darzalex sales in the near term.

J&J collaboration on DARZALEX is expiring soon.

J&J and Genmab have an existing global commercialization agreement in which Genmab granted J&J an exclusive license to develop and sell products containing daratumumab (the active ingredient in DARZALEX) for the treatment of patients with certain indications of multiple myeloma [MM] and light-chain [AL] amyloidosis. In exchange, Genmab receives up to 20% royalties from the net sales of these products paid until the expiration of the last relevant Genmab patents. These patents, after accounting for extensions, begin to expire in 2029 (U.S.), 2030 (Japan) and 2031 (Europe). Because of the nature of the agreement, DARZALEX royalties are projected to decline to near 0 by 2032, the only question is whether Genmab can open up new revenue streams to make up for this loss.

HexaBody-CD38 was a failed succession plan.

Patent expiration is a common risk in the Biotech sector with the potential to dramatically erode a company's revenue and market value. Pharmaceutical companies are typically granted 20-year patents from the point of discovery, which includes research and development time. This effectively leaves most drugs with approximately 10 years of market exclusivity after which competitors can introduce generic versions, often at substantially lower prices, resulting in a steep decline in sales (the "patent cliff"). Pharmaceutical companies often navigate this cliff by developing improved versions of their existing drugs, and proactively transitioning patients to the new formulations before the original patent expires, thereby maintaining their competitive edge and market position.

HexaBody-CD38 (GEN3014) was GenMab’s strategy to replace DARZALEX and maintain revenue streams from the same patient population. HexaBody-CD38 targets the same antibody CD38 as DARZALEX but is designed to be more potent. It uses Genmab’s HexaBody technology to form clusters upon target binding that make it better at mobilizing the complementary immune system to kill myeloma cells with higher potency. In head-to-head clinical trials against DARZALEX, Hexabody-CD38 showed promising results with a better overall response rate (55% versus 52%). But J&J did not find the results sufficient to warrant further investment. The decision made sense as the new drug must be much better than the old drug to convince patients and prescribers to abandon the old drug especially when the old drug gets cheaper with generic versions in the market. While the performance of HexaBody-CD38 demonstrated potential for Genmab’s HexaBody technology in future applications, it failed to succeed as a replacement for DARZALEX and left Genmab still exposed to the patent risk.

Can Existing Products Fill the DARZALEX Gap?

Genmab provided future sales projections for most of its commercialized products. These projections are largely in line with those made by other independent analysts but keep in mind that it shares revenue with collaborators on all its products. DARZALEX, Kesimpta, and TEPEZZA are expected to grow moderately at a high-single-digit compound annual growth rate [CAGR] to about $16.4 billion in 2030. TECVAYLI, TALVEY, RYBREVANT, have a much higher runway with projections assuming double-digit CAGRs but Genmab’s royalties on these products will remain much smaller than DARZALEX.
